Were You Asked This?
Design Slack

Design a messaging platform like Slack that supports real-time messaging through direct messages and channels (public/private), message threads, new message indicators, and persistent message storage.

Asked at:

OpenAI

Coinbase

Databricks

Try This Problem Yourself

Practice with guided hints and real-time feedback

Start Practice

Slack is a real-time team messaging and collaboration platform where people send direct messages, chat in public or private channels, start threads to keep discussions organized, and see indicators when there are new messages. Users expect low-latency delivery, consistent unread markers across devices, and durable history they can search and revisit later.

Interviewers ask this question to probe your ability to design a high-throughput, low-latency, multi-tenant system that mixes real-time delivery with durable storage. It tests how you handle fan-out to many recipients, presence and connection lifecycle, message ordering and idempotency, access control for private channels, and efficient unread/new message indicators at scale.

Hello Interview Problem Breakdown
WhatsApp

System design answer key for designing a messaging application like WhatsApp, built by FAANG managers and staff engineers.

View Answer Key

Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

Users should be able to send and receive real-time messages in direct messages and channels (public and private).

Users should be able to create, join, and manage channels, including membership and privacy settings.

Users should be able to start and reply to threads within channels and DMs to keep conversations organized.

Users should see accurate new message indicators and unread counts across devices, and retrieve full message history at any time.

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

How will you deliver messages in real time with low latency while handling offline clients and reconnections?

How will you implement unread counts and the 'new messages' marker accurately and efficiently?

How do you scale high-fanout channels and threads so a single write doesn’t overload your system?

How will you store messages indefinitely with support for search, edits/deletes, and access control for private channels?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Real-time Updates

Slack is fundamentally a push-based system where users expect near-instant updates. You need a pattern for server-initiated delivery (WebSockets or similar), presence, reconnect, and replay from a cursor to catch clients up after disconnects.

Read More
Scaling Writes

Channels and threads generate sustained write streams, and hot channels create bursty fan-out. A write-optimized, append-first pipeline with durable logs, sharded partitions, and asynchronous fan-out keeps the write path fast and resilient.

Read More
Scaling Reads

Most user time is spent reading message history and catching up. Read-scaling with per-conversation partitions, caches for cursors/unread counts, and efficient range scans is essential to keep latency low and cost predictable.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

Kafka

Kafka fits the append-and-fan-out model: messages are appended to a topic partitioned by conversation or workspace, then consumed by fan-out workers, indexing pipelines, and analytics. It provides ordering within partitions and durable replay for reconnecting clients.

Read More
Redis

Redis excels at low-latency, ephemeral state: presence, per-user cursors, unread counters, and small per-user queues. Pub/sub can power lightweight notifications, while Redis as a cache shields your primary store from hot read/write paths for indicators.

Read More
Cassandra

Cassandra’s wide-column, time-series friendly data model and high write throughput make it a strong fit for durable message storage with per-conversation partitions and ordered clustering. It supports large-scale range reads and multi-datacenter replication.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

WhatsApp

Both systems require real-time messaging, delivery semantics (ordering, idempotency), offline catch-up, and durable storage. Slack adds multi-recipient channels and threads, but the core messaging pipeline and cursor management are closely related.

Read
Practice
FB Live Comments

Live comments highlight high-fanout, low-latency push and hot content contention. Techniques like append-only logs, subscriber fan-out, and backpressure control directly apply to busy Slack channels and threaded replies.

Read
Practice
FB News Feed

Feed systems face fan-out choices (on write vs on read), caching, and per-user views with unread indicators. The trade-offs in precomputation, indexing, and access control map well to Slack’s channel timelines and new message markers.

Read
Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

Broadcasting messages synchronously from the request thread to every recipient

This couples write latency to the slowest consumer and collapses under high fan-out. It risks timeouts, backpressure across the fleet, and poor tail latency; you need an asynchronous fan-out pipeline with buffering and retries.

Using a single primary database for messages, unread counters, and presence

Mixing durable time-series data with hot, ephemeral state causes contention, lock amplification, and scaling bottlenecks. Unread counters and presence belong in fast in-memory stores, while messages go to an append-optimized durable store.

Hand-waving access control for private channels and DMs

Failing to enforce ACLs on both real-time subscriptions and storage reads leads to data leaks. Every subscription, fetch, and search must verify membership; ACL changes must invalidate caches and active streams immediately.

Comments

Comment
Anonymous
Posting as woaidiyia
rutvik patel
Premium
• 10个月前

Is it same as designing whats app?

1

Reply
Phil John
• 6个月前

not quite - WhatsApp doesn't durably store the messages, or offer server-side search (if you look at the deep dive for WhatsApp there is an inbox of messages not yet sent to a give client, which is purged when ACK'd or after 30 days). For slack you need to store the messages either forever, or for a company defined retention period (e.g. 2 years) as they're not stored on the client.

2

Reply
